zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Teresa Díaz II de Haro于1254年之前出生时，她的父亲Diego López III de Haro38岁，母亲Constanza de Béarn8岁。她于大约1270年与Juan Nuñez I "el Gordo" de Lara结婚。他们至少是2个儿子和2个女儿的父母。

nameMeanings.name: Haro
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Jesus, Juan, Luis, Salvador, Manuel, Carlos, Jorge, Miguel, Alberto, Francisco, Jaime.
Spanish: habitational name from a place in the province of La Rioja, so called from a North Castilian form of Spanish faro ‘beacon’. Compare De Haro .
In some cases possibly also French: metonymic occupational name for a peddler, from an Old French cry used to hail someone or to attract attention.

variants.name: De Haro
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Rafael, Arturo, Jaime, Jesus, Ubaldo, Alfredo, Enrique, Felipe, Francisco, Gustavo, Juan.
Spanish: habitational name, formed with the preposition de ‘from’, for someone from a place called Haro (see Haro ).

variants.name: Haros
meanings: Hispanic (mainly Mexico): probably the plural form of Spanish   Haro  .

nameMeanings.name: Teresa
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: form of   Theresa .  In the English-speaking world the name is often chosen in this spelling by Roman Catholics, with particular reference to the Spanish saint, Teresa of Ávila (  Teresa Cepeda de Ahumada ,  1515–82 ).

variants.name: Theresa
meanings: Of problematic origin. The name seems to have been first used in Spain and Portugal, and, according to tradition, was the name of the wife of St Paulinus of Nola, who spent most of his life in Spain; she was said to have originated (and to have derived her name) from the Greek island of Thēra. However, this story is neither factually nor etymologically confirmed.

variants.name: Teresia
meanings: Of problematic origin. The name seems to have been first used in Spain and Portugal, and, according to tradition, was the name of the wife of St Paulinus of Nola, who spent most of his life in Spain; she was said to have originated (and to have derived her name) from the Greek island of Thēra. However, this story is neither factually nor etymologically confirmed.

nameMeanings.name: Díaz
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: ‘Meadows, gardens’; in some countries denoting meadows where horses were broken in.

nameMeanings.name: II
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of the various boys' names with the first syllable Ed-, especially Edward .

variants.name: Edward
meanings: From an Old English personal name derived from ēad ‘prosperity, riches’ + weard ‘guard’. This has been one of the most successful of all Old English names, in frequent use from before the Conquest to the present day, and even being exported into other European languages. It was the name of three Anglo-Saxon kings and has been borne by eight kings of England since the Norman Conquest. It is also the name of the youngest son of Queen Elizabeth II . The most influential early bearer was King Edward the Confessor ( ?1002–66 ; ruled 1042–66 ). In a troubled period of English history, he contrived to rule fairly and (for a time at any rate) firmly. But in the latter part of his reign he paid more attention to his religion than to his kingdom. He died childless, and his death sparked off conflicting claims to his throne, which were resolved by the victory of William the Conqueror at the Battle of Hastings. His memory was honoured by Normans and English alike, for his fairness and his piety. Edward's mother was Norman; he had spent part of his youth in Normandy; and William claimed to have been nominated by Edward as his successor. Edward was canonized in the 12th century, and came to be venerated throughout Europe as a model of a Christian king.

en.lifeSummary: When Teresa Díaz II de Haro was born before 1254, her father, Diego López III de Haro, was 38 and her mother, Constanza de Béarn, was 8. She married Juan Nuñez I "el Gordo" de Lara about 1270. They were the parents of at least 2 sons and 2 daughters.

ko.lifeSummary: Teresa Díaz II de Haro(이)가 1254년 이전에 태어났을 때, 그녀의 아버지 Diego López III de Haro(은)는 38세였고, 그녀의 어머니 Constanza de Béarn(은)는 8세였다. 그녀는 대략 1270년 Juan Nuñez I "el Gordo" de Lara(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 두 명의 아들과 두 명의 딸을 두었다.
